title: From Homeless to MIT Grad: How I Started from $0 and Created Billions
description: In 25 years, Sandeep has learned that success in business comes from the lessons least expected — often from failures rather than wins. In this video, he shares 12 hard-earned insights that took him from being a broke musician to an MIT graduate and a multi-millionaire leading tech companies that have driven over $40 billion in value.
transcript:
these are the 12 insights that took me
from a broke musician to an MIT grad
I've been the CEO and sea level
executive at companies that made over
$60 billion in five exits so let's
launch into it my first Insight is this
if you want to create a Founder's
mindset then you need to stop worrying
about your mission statement a
successful business is born from solving
real problems none of the businesses
that we admire today started with a
Grand Mission to change the world they
started in a garage or a dorm room how
did Microsoft start just two guys
writing software for a computer company
that doesn't even exist today and what
about Facebook Zuckerberg's first crack
at greatness was called face Mash he
made a site so people could rate if
women were hot or not there was no Grand
Mission about connecting the world no
mission statement and what about Theos
or FTX these companies had inspiring
mission and the founders are in prison
right now so solve a problem nail a
product and the mission will follow my
second Insight is to chase problems not
your passion passion is just a hype you
can't build a business on it Airbnb
wasn't started because we all love
sharing our homes it was started by Two
Broke Founders who couldn't afford a
hotel room back in the early 2000s I was
a director at a tech company and the CEO
and the CTO both brilliant people had a
huge passion to build a search engine
and the company at one point was the
fastest growing search engine on the

started eating everybody's lunch and so
we had to Pivot hard so we sold our

build an information retrieval software
for large corporation so from B Toc we
went to B2B eventually Microsoft bought
us for $1.2 billion so it was a good
exit but the point is that we completely
changed our business model our business
focus and your business will change
completely very frequently think of
Google they started from search to
advertising and now ai passions don't
last but problems do next up is number
three success is all about subtraction
Southwest built a giant business on the
subtraction model that's right Southwest
subtracted fancy meals reserved seats
bigger Airport reps and build a huge
business same for Apple but Apple was
just weeks away from bankruptcy what did
Steve Jobs do he cut almost all the
product lines and focused on just core
products like iMac because he knew that
big wins come from doing fewer things
better so if you want to succeed say no
a lot subtraction also clarifies a
Founder's mindset running a business is
overwhelming enough right there's always
a fire that's burning a deal you have to
close an angry customer you have to call
a new hire to interview and so if you're
an entrepreneur or an executive the most
important subtraction is internal Focus
only on the essential and subtract all
the rest of the noise that's what
preserves your sanity that brings us to
Insight number four you need to follow
evidence not your ego confidence looks
sexy but that's not a strategy Google
Nvidia Amazon are successful because
they innovate daily by doing thousands
of experiments I was a sea level exac at
a tech company amazing technology and we
had been selling software with a certain
pricing model and the board wanted
another pricing model called SAS it's a
subscription based pricing model and so
all of us were stuck should we do this
pricing versus that pricing so we just
started doing experiments we picked a
bunch of pricing models with different
customer cohorts we did a lot of AB
testing and we collected a lot of data
and what we found completely surprised
us because what was working was a third
model a third option we hadn't thought
about a volume based pricing model and
the customers loved it and the growth
just took off you know as we become more
successful we started believing in our
own BS don't rely on your intuition
alone let your data show you what's
working and no matter what you do stop
getting guessing because your opinions
aren't going to change the facts Insight
number five Everybody Hurts that means
you have to sell promises shortcuts
Solutions customers don't buy products
they buy better versions of themselves
it's about an emotional connection Nike
is successful because they know how to
sell the dream that's a great example
you know in the'80s Nike wasn't a big
deal no one cared about them Adidas was
the company that defined cool so how did
Nike beat them not by producing better

feeling that's what Air Jordans were
about right everybody wanted to feel
like a champion they wanted to be a part
of something bigger than themselves
that's the emotional connection that's
why Ziploc is not a plastic bag it makes
us feel good that we're reducing waste
apple is not about a piece of glass it
makes us feel sexy like we have great
taste like we have a ride so seller a
promise not a product my sixth Insight
is that everybody lies don't just listen
to your customers watch what they're
doing Henry Ford said if I had asked
people what they wanted they would have
said Faster Horses customers say one
thing and do another I remember when I
was a CEO of an awesome AI company we
sold a powerful AI fraud detection
software and every time our sales rep
would go and talk to a prospect they
would all come back fully pumped because
the prospects loved us they would talk
endlessly about how they wanted to fight
fraud and how Bots were the existential
threat to the internet and on and on but
when it came time to buy nothing so
instead of listening to our customers
praising us we started watching how and
where they were using our product and
that changed our goto Market strategy
completely we started targeting a
different set of customers a different
type of customers who had a higher sense
of urgency and that led to explosive
growth so learn from how your customers
behave what they do speaks louder than
what they say the next Insight is that
if you want to build a great business
nail the founder market fit the best
Founders don't just know their Market
they are their Market Melanie Perkins
didn't just wake up one day and start
kenba she used to work as a design

their pain the solution to that pain

canva they think it's so intuitive
because it came from a very strong
founder market fit so if you want to
start a business find a fit between who
you are and your Market customers will
trust you more because you walked in
their shoes my next Insight is this
without product Market fit you have NOS
business if you just started your
business here are three things I would
do be extremely Scrappy watch your cash
burn and find your product Market fit a
great example is a company that you've
never heard of it was called bourbon
they launched in early 20110 as a
check-in app so you know users could
share their location with their friends
but the founders saw something unusual
the users were using that app mostly to

the entire product Focus solely on photo

the rest as you know is history product
Market fit helps you prove if your

what they love about also learn about a
bunch of metrics like activation rate
retention rate customer churn rate they
will help you figure out how to get to a
solid pmf the next Insight is that
pricing is not just about driving
Revenue it's about finding what the

give away things for free free isn't a
business model is just a hobby Freeman
products used to be fashionable ones but
a lot of startups eventually found out
painfully of course that when they
started charging their 90% retention
rate plummeted to 10% well guess what
they never had a strong Market fit in

willingness to pay very early on do as
many pricing experiments as you can
right I mean find the right price point
where you can make enough money but your
customers feel like they want if your
customers are willing to pay for it then
you know your product and services are
delivering value that brings us to our
number 10 who you hire in your team
decides if you win or lose I only care
about this one thing when I am hiring
finding people who can adapt to change I
don't remember being in a company that
did not change its direction every
single company that I was at ended up
doing something different in 18 months
after I joined the product changed the
market reality changed the leadership
changed the investors changed of course
my role would change and it got a roll
with the punches and adapt no one knows
what any business would look like in 3

these are the people you need to take

which is not very sexy but it is
essential you need processes let's talk

a CEO I remember doing a standup meeting

just just for 10 minutes and on top of

executive every week and we had an
executive meeting with all of them
together for 2 hours every Monday and we
had an all hands where the entire

talk about what's going on why that
matters so too many meetings not at all
you know companies today run at a crazy
pace and as a leader as a CEO as a
Founder it's your job to overcome
communicate that way anyone can make a
decision when you're not in the room the
second process is goal setting and one
book I would recommend is measure What
Matters by John door don't reinvent the
wheel at all when it comes to goal
setting just use okrs okr stands for
objectives and key results every company
I know uses them so create maybe three
or four okrs per quarter and then cast
skate them down to the function to the
team to an individual that way everybody
is rowing in the same direction and the
ship moves faster Insight number 12 is
that all your business fundamentals come
down to just one thing cash you need to
understand this about money a very
sophisticated CEO I once knew called me
and I was in San Francisco in a cab and
he says suep I'm going to resign and my

going on and he said some Silicon Valley
Bank had swept $10 million from the
company's bank account because of some

was that $10 million just disappeared
overnight because his Chief Financial
Officer didn't keep track of the cash
account now they did Bridge financing
they survived but they never fully
recovered and the unfortunate thing here
was that nothing was illegal there there
was no embezzlement no one was cooking
the books just lack of process lack of
oversight so remember revenue is not
cash operating margin is not cash profit
is not cash only cash is real the rest
is accounting it is the vital oxygen for
your business keep track of your oxygen
and here's my one bonus Insight it's
controversial and I learned it way later

lead with love take care of your people
your product and your profits in that
order your job is not to be the boss but

they will surprise you I've seen this
